task: Audacity is open with /home/user/Music/audiobook.aup loaded. The project contains a single wavetrack (5 seconds of narration) and no label tracks. Add a label track and place four chapter labels on it, in this order from earliest to latest: 'Intro', 'Chapter 1', 'Chapter 2', 'Outro'. Save the project so the label track and its four labels are persisted to /home/user/Music/audiobook.aup. Exact timestamps do not matter — only that there are exactly four labels with those exact titles on one label track.

cli_feasibility.translation: 1. cli-anything-audacity project new -o /home/user/Music/audiobook.json 2. cli-anything-audacity --project /home/user/Music/audiobook.json label add 0.5 Intro 3. cli-anything-audacity --project /home/user/Music/audiobook.json label add 1.5 'Chapter 1' 4. cli-anything-audacity --project /home/user/Music/audiobook.json label add 2.5 'Chapter 2' 5. cli-anything-audacity --project /home/user/Music/audiobook.json label add 4.0 Outro 6. cli-anything-audacity --project /home/user/Music/audiobook.json project save
cli_feasibility.reason: CLI-Anything-Audacity supports label management via 'label add' command which can add labels at specific time positions with titles. Project operations are fully supported.

task:
Open the file /home/user/Documents/quarters.xlsx in LibreOffice Calc. The workbook has sheets Q1, Q2, Q3, Q4 (each with Product in column A and Sales in column B for 8 products in rows 2-9) and a 'PriorYear' sheet with the same 8 products and their prior-year totals in column B.

1. Create a new sheet named 'Annual'.
2. On Annual, fill row 1 with bold headers: A1='Product', B1='Annual', C1='Prior Year', D1='YoY %'.
3. In A2:A9 copy the 8 product names from Q1.A2:A9 (Alpha, Beta, Gamma, Delta, Epsilon, Zeta, Eta, Theta).
4. In B2 enter =Q1.B2+Q2.B2+Q3.B2+Q4.B2 (or use =SUM(Q1.B2,Q2.B2,Q3.B2,Q4.B2)) and fill down through B9.
5. In C2 enter =VLOOKUP(A2,PriorYear.A2:B9,2,0) and fill down through C9.
6. In D2 enter =(B2-C2)/C2*100 and fill down through D9.
7. In A10 type 'Total' and make it bold. In B10 enter =SUM(B2:B9). In C10 enter =SUM(C2:C9).
8. Save the file.

verification.eval: result.get('formula') is not None and 'Q1' in result.get('formula','').upper() and 'Q4' in result.get('formula','').upper()
verification.description: Annual B2 references Q1 and Q4

verification.eval: result.get('formula') is not None and ('VLOOKUP' in result.get('formula','').upper() or 'INDEX' in result.get('formula','').upper() or 'PRIORYEAR' in result.get('formula','').upper())
verification.description: Annual C2 pulls prior year via lookup or direct reference

task:
Open the file /home/user/Documents/attendance.xlsx in LibreOffice Calc. It contains a sheet called 'Attendance' with 20 students (rows 2-21) and 20 class dates (columns B-U). Row 1 has headers: Student Name (A), then date headers (B1 through U1). Each cell in the attendance grid contains 'P' (present), 'A' (absent), or 'L' (late).

1. In cell V1, type 'Days Present' and make it bold.
2. In cell W1, type 'Days Absent' and make it bold.
3. In cell X1, type 'Days Late' and make it bold.
4. In cell Y1, type 'Attendance %' and make it bold.
5. In V2, enter the formula =COUNTIF(B2:U2,"P") to count how many days Olivia Parker was present.
6. In W2, enter the formula =COUNTIF(B2:U2,"A") to count absent days.
7. In X2, enter the formula =COUNTIF(B2:U2,"L") to count late days.
8. In Y2, enter the formula =V2/20*100 to calculate attendance percentage.
9. Copy all four formulas (V2:Y2) down through rows 3-21 for all 20 students.
10. In cell A22, type 'Class Average' and make it bold.
11. In V22, enter the formula =AVERAGE(V2:V21) to compute the average days present across all students.
12. In Y22, enter the formula =AVERAGE(Y2:Y21) to compute the average attendance percentage.
13. Save the file.

verification.eval: result.get('formula') is not None and 'COUNTIF' in result.get('formula','').upper()

task:
Open the file /home/user/Documents/budget.xlsx in LibreOffice Calc. It contains a sheet called 'January' with columns: Category (A), Planned (B), Actual (C). Row 1 has headers (bold), rows 2-9 have 8 expense categories: Rent, Utilities, Groceries, Transport, Insurance, Entertainment, Healthcare, Savings.

1. Create a new sheet called 'February'. In row 1, add the same headers: A1='Category', B1='Planned', C1='Actual'. Copy the 8 category names from January A2:A9 into February A2:A9. Leave the Planned and Actual columns in February empty (the agent just needs to set up the structure).
2. Create a new sheet called 'March' with the same structure as February (headers in row 1, category names in A2:A9).
3. Create a new sheet called 'Q1 Summary'.
4. In the Q1 Summary sheet:
   - A1: 'Category' (bold)
   - B1: 'Total Planned' (bold)
   - C1: 'Total Actual' (bold)
   - D1: 'Difference' (bold)
5. Copy the 8 category names from January A2:A9 into Q1 Summary A2:A9.
6. In Q1 Summary B2, enter the formula =January.B2+February.B2+March.B2 to sum planned amounts across all three months.
7. Copy this formula pattern down through B3:B9 for all categories.
8. In Q1 Summary C2:C9, enter similar formulas summing the Actual columns across all three months (e.g., C2 = =January.C2+February.C2+March.C2).
9. In Q1 Summary D2:D9, enter formulas computing the difference: D2 = =B2-C2 (Total Planned minus Total Actual).
10. Save the file.

verification.eval: result.get('formula') is not None and 'January' in result.get('formula','') or 'JANUARY' in result.get('formula','').upper()
verification.description: Q1 Summary B2 contains a cross-sheet formula referencing January
Q1 Summary B2 = 1500 (January Rent planned, Feb+Mar are 0)

verification.eval: result.get('formula') is not None and ('B2' in result.get('formula','').upper() and 'C2' in result.get('formula','').upper())
verification.description: Q1 Summary D2 contains formula referencing B2 and C2

task:
Open the file /home/user/Documents/roster.xlsx in LibreOffice Calc. The sheet 'Roster' has bold headers in row 1 (A='Student ID', B='Name', C='Exam1', D='Exam2', E='Exam3') and 25 students in rows 2-26 with numeric scores 0-100.

Compute weighted final grades, assign letter grades, and summarize the distribution:

1. In F1 type 'Final' (bold). In G1 type 'Grade' (bold).
2. In F2:F26 enter =C2*0.3+D2*0.3+E2*0.4 (30% Exam1, 30% Exam2, 40% Exam3).
3. In G2:G26 enter a nested IF formula that assigns a letter grade from the Final: 'A' if >=90, else 'B' if >=80, else 'C' if >=70, else 'D' if >=60, else 'F'. Use: =IF(F2>=90,"A",IF(F2>=80,"B",IF(F2>=70,"C",IF(F2>=60,"D","F")))).
4. Create a new sheet named 'GradeSummary'.
5. On GradeSummary, in row 1 enter bold headers: A1='Grade', B1='Count'.
6. In A2:A6 enter 'A', 'B', 'C', 'D', 'F' (in that order).
7. In B2:B6 enter =COUNTIF(Roster.G2:G26,A2) (and fill down).
8. Save the file.

verification.eval: result.get('formula') is not None and '0.3' in result.get('formula','') and '0.4' in result.get('formula','')
result.get('formula') is not None and 'IF' in result.get('formula','').upper()
result.get('formula') is not None and 'COUNTIF' in result.get('formula','').upper()

task:
Open the file /home/user/Documents/competition.xlsx in LibreOffice Calc. Sheet 'Results' has Name (A), Country (B), Score (C) with 20 competitors in rows 2-21.

1. In D1 type 'Rank' and make it bold. In E1 type 'Percentile' and make it bold. In F1 type 'Medal' and make it bold.
2. In D2 enter =RANK(C2,C$2:C$21) and fill down through D21. Higher scores should get rank 1.
3. In E2 enter =PERCENTRANK(C$2:C$21,C2) and fill down through E21.
4. In F2 enter a nested-IF that returns 'Gold' when the rank is 1, 'Silver' when 2, 'Bronze' when 3, 'Finalist' when rank is 4 or 5, and empty string otherwise: =IF(D2=1,"Gold",IF(D2=2,"Silver",IF(D2=3,"Bronze",IF(D2<=5,"Finalist","")))). Fill down through F21.
5. In A23 fill in 'Top Country' and make it bold.
6. In B23 enter =INDEX(B2:B21,MATCH(1,D2:D21,0)) to return the country of the rank-1 competitor.
7. Save the file.

verification.eval: result.get('formula') is not None and 'RANK' in result.get('formula','').upper()
result.get('formula') is not None and 'PERCENTRANK' in result.get('formula','').upper()
result.get('formula') is not None and ('INDEX' in result.get('formula','').upper() or 'VLOOKUP' in result.get('formula','').upper())

task:
Open the file /home/user/Documents/regional_sales.xlsx in LibreOffice Calc. The sheet 'Sales' has bold headers in row 1 (A='Region', B='Q1', C='Q2', D='Q3', E='Q4', F='Total') and 12 regions in rows 2-13. The Total column in F2:F13 already has =SUM(B2:E2) formulas.

Apply two conditional-formatting rules:

1. Select range B2:E13 and apply a 'Color Scale' conditional format so that low values shade red and high values shade green. A 3-color or 2-color red-to-green scale is fine.

2. Select range F2:F13 and add a 'Condition' rule of type 'Cell value is > 100000'. Apply a cell style that uses a RED font color and BOLD weight for matching cells (you can create a new style called 'HighTotal' for this, or reuse 'Bad').

Open the file /home/user/Documents/contacts.xlsx in LibreOffice Calc. The sheet 'Contacts' has bold headers in row 1 (A='Full Name', B='Email', C='Phone') and 25 contact rows in rows 2-26. Several emails have mixed case or trailing whitespace, and some are duplicates of each other.

Clean and annotate the list:

1. In D1 type 'First Name' (bold). In E1 type 'Last Name' (bold). In F1 type 'Clean Email' (bold). In G1 type 'Is Duplicate' (bold).
2. In D2:D26 extract the first name using =TRIM(LEFT(A2,FIND(" ",A2)-1)).
3. In E2:E26 extract the last name using =TRIM(MID(A2,FIND(" ",A2)+1,100)).
4. In F2:F26 produce a clean email with =LOWER(TRIM(B2)).
5. In G2:G26 mark duplicates of the cleaned email with =IF(COUNTIF($F$2:$F$26,F2)>1,"Yes","No").
6. Save the file.

Open the file /home/user/Documents/loan.xlsx in LibreOffice Calc. The sheet 'Loan' has loan inputs in B1:B3 (Principal=24000, Annual Rate=0.06, Term Months=12) and schedule headers already in row 6 (Month, Payment, Interest, Principal, Balance).

1. In cell C1 enter the formula =PMT(B2/12,B3,-B1) so the single monthly payment is shown. Make C1 bold.
2. Fill rows 7 through 18 with month numbers 1..12 in column A.
3. In rows 7 through 18, fill column B (Payment) with =-PMT(B$2/12,B$3,-B$1) so every row shows the same monthly payment (positive number, about 2065.52).
4. In rows 7 through 18, fill column C (Interest) with =-IPMT(B$2/12,A7,B$3,-B$1) style formulas (row reference must use the month number in column A).
5. In rows 7 through 18, fill column D (Principal) with =-PPMT(B$2/12,A7,B$3,-B$1) style formulas.
6. In rows 7 through 18, fill column E (Balance) as the running remaining balance. E7 should be =B1-D7 and each subsequent row should be =E[prev]-D[current], so E18 ends at 0.
7. In A19 type 'Totals' and make it bold.
8. In B19 enter =SUM(B7:B18). In C19 enter =SUM(C7:C18). In D19 enter =SUM(D7:D18).
9. Save the file .

6. In H2 enter =F2-G2 for net pay. Fill down through H13.
7. In A14 type 'Totals' and make it bold. In D14 enter =SUM(D2:D13). In E14 enter =SUM(E2:E13). In F14 enter =SUM(F2:F13). In G14 enter =SUM(G2:G13). In H14 enter =SUM(H2:H13).
8. Save the file .

task:
Open the file /home/user/Documents/catalog.xlsx in LibreOffice Calc.

The workbook has two sheets:
- 'Catalog': bold headers in row 1 (A='SKU', B='Product', C='Units Sold', D='List Price') and 25 products in rows 2-26.
- 'Tiers': bold headers in row 1 (A='Min Units', B='Tier', C='Discount'), with rows 2-5 defining the tiers sorted ascending: (0, 'Bronze', 0), (100, 'Silver', 0.05), (500, 'Gold', 0.10), (1000, 'Platinum', 0.15).

Build a discounted-price breakdown and a tier-level summary:

1. On Catalog, in row 1 add bold headers E1='Tier', F1='Discounted Price', G1='Revenue'.
2. In E2:E26 use =VLOOKUP(C2,Tiers.A:C,2,1) to fetch the Tier name for each row (approximate-match VLOOKUP against the ascending Min Units column).
3. In F2:F26 use =D2*(1-VLOOKUP(C2,Tiers.A:C,3,1)) to compute the per-unit discounted price.
4. In G2:G26 enter =C2*F2 (revenue = units sold * discounted price).
5. Create a new sheet named 'TierSummary'.
6. On TierSummary, in row 1 add bold headers A1='Tier', B1='Revenue'. In A2:A5 enter 'Bronze', 'Silver', 'Gold', 'Platinum' (in that order).
7. In B2:B5 enter =SUMIFS(Catalog.G:G,Catalog.E:E,A2) (revenue totalled per tier).
8. In D1 add bold header 'Best Tier'. In D2 enter =INDEX(A2:A5,MATCH(MAX(B2:B5),B2:B5,0)) so D2 returns the tier name with the highest revenue.
9. Save the file .

verification.eval: result.get('formula') is not None and 'VLOOKUP' in result.get('formula','').upper()
result.get('formula') is not None and 'VLOOKUP' in result.get('formula','').upper()
result.get('formula') is not None and 'SUMIF' in result.get('formula','').upper()
result.get('formula') is not None and 'INDEX' in result.get('formula','').upper() and 'MATCH' in result.get('formula','').upper()

2. In cell E1, enter 'Commission Amount' and make it bold.
3. In D2, enter a nested IF formula to determine the commission rate based on sales tiers:
   - If Monthly Sales (C2) > 20000, the rate is 10% (0.10)
   - If Monthly Sales (C2) >= 10000, the rate is 8% (0.08)
   - Otherwise, the rate is 5% (0.05)
   The formula should be: =IF(C2>20000,0.1,IF(C2>=10000,0.08,0.05))
4. In E2, enter the formula =C2*D2 to calculate the commission amount.
5. Copy both formulas (D2:E2) down through rows 3-21 for all 20 records.
6. Create a new sheet called 'Commission Summary'.
7. In the Commission Summary sheet:
   - A1: 'Metric' (bold)
   - B1: 'Value' (bold)
   - A2: 'Total Sales'
   - B2: =SUM(Sales.C2:C21)
   - A3: 'Total Commission'
   - B3: =SUM(Sales.E2:E21)
   - A4: 'Average Commission Rate'
   - B4: =B3/B2 (this should yield the weighted average rate)
8. Save the file .

verification.eval: result.get('formula') is not None and 'IF' in result.get('formula','').upper()
result.get('formula') is not None and ('C2' in result.get('formula','').upper() and 'D2' in result.get('formula','').upper())
verification.description: E2 formula references C2 and D2

verification.eval: result.get('formula') is not None and 'SUM' in result.get('formula','').upper()
verification.description: Commission Summary B2 contains a SUM formula

verification.description: Commission Summary B4 formula references B3 and B2

task:
Open /home/user/godot_projects/class_game/scripts/enemy.gd. It is currently an empty stub. Replace its contents with a valid GDScript class that satisfies all of the following:

1. Declares class_name Enemy
2. extends CharacterBody2D
3. Declares a signal named died
4. Has an @export var max_health: int = 100
5. Defines a function named take_damage that takes one parameter amount: int

An example implementation:

class_name Enemy
extends CharacterBody2D

signal died

@export var max_health: int = 100

var current_health: int = max_health

func take_damage(amount: int):
	current_health -= amount
	if current_health <= 0:
		died.emit()

Save the file.

task:
Open LibreOffice Impress.

1、Then set 'Always save as' for 'Presentation' to 'PowerPoint 2007-365 (.pptx)', enable 'Always create backup copy', enable 'Save AutoRecovery information every', and set the AutoRecovery interval to 1 minute.

2. Change 'Measurement unit' to 'Inch'.

After confirming all dialogs, make sure the preferences are flushed to ~/.config/libreoffice/4/user/registrymodifications.xcu. The verifier cannot read the preferences until LibreOffice has exited cleanly.

task:
MuseScore 3 is already open with the file /home/user/Documents/melody.mscz. The score has a single piano staff with 8 quarter notes (2 measures, 4 notes each) in 4/4 time and currently has no lyrics.

Perform these steps:
1. Click the very first note of the melody to select it.
2. Press Ctrl+L to begin adding lyrics on that note.
3. Type the word 'hello' attached to the first note (type 'hello' and then press space to advance — this will write 'hello' as a single syllable under the first note).
4. Type 'world' under the second note and press space.
5. Type 'sing' under the third note and press space.
6. Type 'out' under the fourth note.
7. Press Escape to exit lyric entry mode.
8. Save the file with Ctrl+S.

The file must still be at /home/user/Documents/melody.mscz. After saving, the score must contain at least one lyric syllable and one of the lyric syllables must contain the substring 'hel' (from 'hello').

verification.description: Score contains at least one lyric syllable
A lyric syllable contains the substring 'hel'

cli_feasibility.translation: Unzip .mscz file, parse XML, find specific <Note> elements by measure/position, insert <Lyrics><text>hello</text></Lyrics> elements, repackage
cli_feasibility.reason: Adding lyrics is inserting child XML elements into Note elements - straightforward with ElementTree, ~35 lines of Python

task:
MuseScore 3 is already open with the file /home/user/Documents/waltz_draft.mscz. The score currently has 4 measures in C major (no sharps or flats), 4/4 time, a first-measure tempo of 120 BPM, and an empty composer.

Rebuild the score header so it reads as a D-major waltz. Make all four of the following changes, then save:
1. Change the time signature at measure 1 to 3/4. Use the Palettes panel (View > Palettes), expand Time Signatures, select measure 1, and apply 3/4.
2. Change the first tempo marking to exactly 108 BPM (quarter note = 108). Edit the existing tempo text in place or replace it via Add > Text > Tempo Marking set to 108.
3. Change the key signature at the start to D major (two sharps). Expand the Key Signatures palette, select measure 1, and apply the 2-sharps key signature.
4. Open File > Score Properties and set the Composer field to exactly: Yvette Chauvire. Click OK.

Then save the file with Ctrl+S.

After saving, the first TimeSig must equal 3/4, the first Tempo must equal 108 BPM, the first KeySig accidental count must equal 2, and the composer metaTag must equal 'Yvette Chauvire'. The file must stay at /home/user/Documents/waltz_draft.mscz.

verification.description: First KeySig has 2 accidentals (D major, two sharps)

cli_feasibility.translation: Unzip .mscz file, parse XML, modify TimeSig (3/4), Tempo (108 BPM), Key (D major), and metaTag (composer), repackage
cli_feasibility.reason: Score header changes are modifications to existing XML elements - can be done with Python stdlib in ~40 lines

task:
MuseScore 3 is already open with the placeholder file /home/user/Documents/placeholder.mscz. You do not need to edit the placeholder score itself — this task is only about changing the MuseScore 3 application preferences.

Perform these steps:
1. Open the Edit menu and click Preferences....
2. Go to the Save section / tab (it may be called 'General > Save' or 'I/O' depending on the layout).
3. Make sure 'Auto Save' is checked (enabled).
4. Set 'Save every ___ minutes' (the Auto Save interval) to exactly 7.
5. Click OK to close the Preferences dialog. This writes the change to ~/.config/MuseScore/MuseScore3.ini.

You do not need to save the placeholder score. After the preferences dialog closes, the autoSave key must be 'true' and the autoSaveInterval key must be '7' in the INI file.

Auto Save interval is set to 7 minutes

cli_feasibility.translation: Directly edit ~/.config/MuseScore/MuseScore3.ini using bash or Python

task:

task: Zotero is installed and a pre-seeded library is at /home/user/Zotero/zotero.sqlite. Launch Zotero. The item 'BERT: Pre-training of Deep Bidirectional Transformers' currently belongs to the 'Transformers' subcollection (under AI Research). Perform a MOVE: (1) Create a new top-level collection named exactly 'Language Models' under My Library. (2) Add the BERT item to the new 'Language Models' collection. (3) Remove BERT from the 'Transformers' collection. After all three steps, BERT must be in 'Language Models' and must NOT be in 'Transformers'. Leave Zotero running.

verification.description: BERT item still exists in library

cli_feasibility.translation: cli-anything-zotero --json collection create 'Language Models' --experimental && cli-anything-zotero --json item find "BERT" && cli-anything-zotero --json item move-to-collection <item_key> 'Language Models' --from Transformers --experimental
cli_feasibility.reason: CLI-Anything supports moving items between collections via the experimental 'item move-to-collection' command. This creates the target collection, adds the item to it, and removes it from the source collection. Requires Zotero to be closed.

task: Zotero is installed and a pre-seeded library is at /home/user/Zotero/zotero.sqlite. Launch Zotero. Locate the item titled 'A Survey of RL Methods' in the middle pane and move it to Trash. Do NOT empty the trash. After the action, the item should still exist in Zotero's Trash folder but should no longer be counted among the 4 regular library items - only 3 regular items should remain. Leave Zotero running.

cli_feasibility.translation: Python script using SQLite: 1) Find item by title, 2) Insert itemID into deletedItems table
cli_feasibility.reason: Zotero's trash functionality is implemented via the deletedItems table - items in this table are considered 'in trash'. A Python script can insert the itemID into deletedItems to move it to trash. This is exactly how Zotero implements deletion.

task: Zotero is installed and a pre-seeded library is at /home/user/Zotero/zotero.sqlite. Launch Zotero. In the collections pane there is an existing top-level collection named 'Reinforcement Learning' which contains the item 'A Survey of RL Methods'. Change the collection name to exactly 'RL Papers'. After renaming, the collection 'Reinforcement Learning' must NOT exist any more, the collection 'RL Papers' must exist, and the survey item must still belong to 'RL Papers'. Leave Zotero running.

verification.description: Survey item is in renamed collection

cli_feasibility.translation: Python script using SQLite: 1) Find collection by name, 2) Update collectionName field in collections table
cli_feasibility.reason: Collection names are stored in the collections table. A Python script can directly update the collectionName field via SQL UPDATE. This is a simple database operation that changes the collection name without affecting its items or subcollections.

task: Zotero is installed and a pre-seeded library is at /home/user/Zotero/zotero.sqlite. The library currently has 4 active items in My Library plus one item in the Trash titled 'Generative Adversarial Networks'. Launch Zotero, click the 'Trash' entry in the left collections pane, locate the item 'Generative Adversarial Networks', and restore it to the library. After the restore the GAN item must NOT be in the trash, must exist among regular items, and the regular (non-trashed) item count must equal 5. Leave Zotero running.

verification.description: GAN item no longer in trash
GAN item exists in regular library

cli_feasibility.translation: Python script using SQLite: 1) Find item by title in deletedItems, 2) Delete the record from deletedItems table
cli_feasibility.reason: Restoring from trash is simply removing the itemID from the deletedItems table. A Python script can DELETE the record from deletedItems, which makes the item appear in the regular library again. This is the reverse of moving to trash.

task: Zotero is installed and a pre-seeded library is at /home/user/Zotero/zotero.sqlite. Launch Zotero. In the collections pane under 'My Library' there is an existing collection named 'AI Research'. Create a subcollection named exactly 'Neural Networks' as a direct child of 'AI Research', not a sibling. Leave Zotero running so the new subcollection is saved.

verification.description: 'Neural Networks' is a subcollection of 'AI Research'

cli_feasibility.translation: cli-anything-zotero --json collection find 'AI Research' && cli-anything-zotero --json collection create 'Neural Networks' --parent <parent_collection_key> --experimental
cli_feasibility.reason: CLI-Anything supports creating subcollections via the experimental 'collection create' command with a --parent parameter. First find the parent collection key, then create the subcollection. Requires Zotero to be closed.

task: Zotero is installed and a pre-seeded library is at /home/user/Zotero/zotero.sqlite. Launch Zotero. The library already has a tag named 'important' (used on one other item). Find the item whose title starts with 'BERT' (full title: 'BERT: Pre-training of Deep Bidirectional Transformers') and add the existing 'important' tag to it via the Tags tab on the right-hand pane. You can pick the tag from autocomplete so no new tag is created. Leave Zotero running.

cli_feasibility.translation: Python script using SQLite: 1) Find BERT item by title, 2) Find 'important' tag in tags table, 3) Insert record into itemTags table linking the item to the tag
cli_feasibility.reason: Adding an existing tag to an item is a simple junction table operation. The tag already exists in the tags table, so the script just needs to insert a record into itemTags linking the itemID and tagID. This is how Zotero implements item-tag relationships.

task: Zotero is installed and a pre-seeded library is at /home/user/Zotero/zotero.sqlite. Launch Zotero. In the library you will find an item titled 'Attention Is All You Need'. Open that item (click it) and add a new tag named exactly 'to-read' (lowercase, with a hyphen) via the Tags tab on the right. The tag does not exist yet — creating it on the item will also create it in the library. Leave Zotero running so the tag is saved.

verification.command: check-item-tag 'Attention Is All You Need' to-read
verification.description: Attention Is All You Need item has tag 'to-read'

cli_feasibility.translation: Python script using SQLite: 1) Insert new 'to-read' tag into tags table, 2) Find item by title, 3) Insert record into itemTags table linking the item to the new tag
cli_feasibility.reason: Creating a new tag and adding it to an item involves two operations: inserting the tag into the tags table, then creating the item-tag relationship in the itemTags junction table. Both are standard SQL operations.
